intro.title: The Most Important Thing You Can Do for Adelina
intro.body:
You're here because you love Adelina. That's the whole reason any of this matters.

This isn't a quiz about being a perfect grandparent — or about doing it 'right.' It's about something much simpler: what does the research actually show about what helps babies like Adelina grow up happy, confident, and close to the people who love them?

In the last 30 years, scientists have learned more about how babies' brains develop than in all of human history before that. Some of what they found confirms what grandparents have always known. Some of it is genuinely surprising — even to pediatricians.

The questions ahead focus on behaviors — small, everyday moments — and what those moments actually do for Adelina over the long run. Answer honestly. There are no wrong people here, only new information.

Let's go.

questions.question: Imagine Adelina is crying and you can't immediately figure out why. Which response is most likely to help her calm down?
questions.answer: B) Get close, make eye contact, and say something like 'I hear you, I'm right here' — even if you don't know why she's upset yet
options: A) Say 'You're okay, you're okay' in an upbeat voice to reassure her
B) Get close, make eye contact, and say something like 'I hear you, I'm right here' — even if you don't know why she's upset yet
C) Give her a few minutes alone to settle herself down
D) Distract her with a toy or a funny face to redirect her attention
questions.follow_up_context: Options A and D are the typical reactions when a baby is crying. However, they skip the baby's experience, which causes her nervous system to register a mismatch. What actually calms a baby's stress response (measured in brain scans) is the feeling of being *seen* — a familiar face, a calm voice, the sense that someone is *with* her in the experience. She doesn't need the problem solved immediately. She needs to know she's not alone.

questions.question:
In 1975, researchers at the University of Minnesota began following a group of babies all the way into adulthood — tracking their friendships, mental health, confidence, and ability to handle life's hard moments decades later.

What turned out to be the single biggest predictor of how well those kids did as adults?

Note: This factor mattered more than how smart they were, and more than how much money their family had.
questions.answer: C) How reliably and warmly their caregivers responded to them as babies
options: A) How intelligent they were
B) Whether their family had financial stability
C) How reliably and warmly their caregivers responded to them as babies
D) Whether they had siblings
questions.follow_up_context:
Led by Dr. Alan Sroufe, this study — one of the longest of its kind ever conducted — followed the same people from birth through their 30s. The factor that predicted healthy adult relationships, emotional resilience, and overall wellbeing above almost anything else was simple: did a trusted adult show up for them, consistently and warmly, when they were babies?

Not perfectly. Not with any special technique. Just: reliably there.

Wealth mattered less. IQ mattered less. Every time you respond to Adelina — every time you pick her up, look her in the eyes, or say 'I've got you' — you are making a deposit into something that will pay off for the rest of her life.

questions.question: True or False: The faster and more consistently you respond to Adelina's crying now, the more clingy and dependent she will be as a toddler.
questions.answer: False — and the research points the other direction entirely
options: True
False
questions.follow_up_context:
This is one of the most common and understandable worries grandparents have — and one of the most thoroughly disproven by research. Researchers Bell and Ainsworth tracked babies in 1972 (with the study replicated many times since) and found that babies whose cries were answered promptly and consistently in their first year actually cried *less* by the end of that year — not more. And at ages 1, 2, and 3, they showed *greater* independence than babies whose cries went unanswered.

Here's the intuition behind it: a baby who learns that the world responds to her doesn't need to escalate to be heard. She's been shown, over and over, that help comes. That trust is what eventually lets her venture out on her own — because she knows someone is there if she needs them.

You cannot spoil Adelina by showing up for her. What you're actually building is confidence.

questions.question:
When Adelina is upset and crying, which of these phrases is most likely to actually calm her down — according to brain research?

(Pick the one that feels most natural to say.)
questions.answer: C) 'You're really upset right now. I'm right here with you.'
options: A) 'Shhh, shhh, it's okay, stop crying'
B) 'Oh look — look at this! Look at the doggie!'
C) 'You're really upset right now. I'm right here with you.'
D) 'There's nothing to cry about, you're fine'
questions.follow_up_context:
UCLA neuroscientist Dr. Matthew Lieberman used brain imaging to study what happens when you put a name to a feeling out loud — and found that it literally reduces activity in the amygdala, the brain's alarm center. Less alarm, less distress, more calm.

Dr. Daniel Siegel, a Harvard-trained psychiatrist, calls this 'name it to tame it.'

Here's the important part for Adelina specifically: she doesn't need to understand the words yet for this to work. What her nervous system picks up is your tone, your pace, your calm — and the fact that you're *with* her rather than trying to rush her through the feeling. That signal — 'you're not alone in this' — is what actually turns the alarm down.

Saying 'you're fine' when she's clearly not sends a small mixed message her body notices, even when her brain can't name it. 'I see that you're upset' honors what's real — and that's what helps.

questions.question: True or False: When Adelina cries at 5 months old, she might just be doing it for attention — trying to get someone to pick her up.
questions.answer: False — a 5-month-old cannot cry 'just for attention' the way an older child might
options: True
False
questions.follow_up_context:
This one surprises a lot of people — because it feels so intuitive that babies 'learn' to cry for attention. And they do, eventually. But not at 5 months.

At this age, a baby's brain doesn't yet have the wiring to think: 'If I cry, they'll come.' That kind of cause-and-effect reasoning develops much later. At 5 months, crying is purely instinctual. It always means something: hunger, discomfort, overstimulation, tiredness, or the need to feel close to someone.

There is no such thing as a manipulative 5-month-old. When Adelina cries, she is communicating. Every time you respond, you are teaching her two things at once: the world is safe, and *you* are someone she can count on.

questions.question:
Picture this: You and Adelina are having a great moment — she's looking right at you, cooing, totally locked in. Then your phone buzzes and you glance down, going quiet and still for about 30 seconds.

What does research suggest Adelina will do?
questions.answer: B) She'll notice almost immediately and try hard to get your attention back — and may get visibly distressed if she can't
options: A) She'll move on and entertain herself — babies are adaptable
B) She'll notice almost immediately and try hard to get your attention back — and may get visibly distressed if she can't
C) She won't notice for at least a few minutes
D) She'll use the quiet time to look around and explore on her own
questions.follow_up_context:
This is based on one of the most famous experiments in infant psychology, called the Still Face Experiment, designed by Dr. Ed Tronick in 1975 and replicated hundreds of times since.

In the experiment, a mother playing warmly with her baby was instructed to suddenly go still and expressionless — like someone staring at a phone. Within seconds, babies as young as 2 months tried everything to bring her back: bigger smiles, pointing, vocalizing. When she didn't respond, they became distressed — turning away, slumping, crying.

The other finding that surprised researchers: even after the mother re-engaged warmly, it took a little while for the baby to recover and trust the connection again.

This isn't about phones being bad. It's a window into how intensely tuned in to *you* Adelina already is. Your face, your attention, your presence — these are her whole world right now. And that is actually a beautiful thing.

questions.question: When Adelina is having a really hard time — inconsolable, really worked up — what does she need most from you in that moment?
questions.answer: B) Your calm. She literally borrows your steadiness to help settle her own nervous system.
options: A) Quiet and space — overstimulation may be the problem
B) Your calm. She literally borrows your steadiness to help settle her own nervous system.
C) Something new to focus on — a change of scenery
D) To be put down so she learns to soothe herself
questions.follow_up_context:
Babies are born without the neurological wiring to calm themselves down — that part of the brain isn't developed yet and won't be for years. In the meantime, they do something remarkable: they *borrow* regulation from the adults around them.

This is called co-regulation, and it works like this: your nervous system and hers are in constant quiet communication. When you hold her with a steady, unhurried calm — slow breathing, a low soft voice, a firm gentle hold — her stress hormones actually begin to decrease in response to yours. Your body is talking to her body.

The flip side is also true: if you tense up when she cries, she feels that and it can make things worse.

You don't have to fix it immediately. You don't have to have the answer. You just have to be *calm with her in it.* That is the most powerful thing you can offer — and it works.

questions.question: True or False: Holding Adelina a lot, cuddling her when she cries, and keeping her close will make her too dependent and slow down her development of independence.
questions.answer: False — consistent closeness in infancy actually predicts more independence later, not less
options: True
False
questions.follow_up_context:
The instinct behind this worry is completely understandable — it feels logical that more holding creates more need for holding. But the research consistently shows the opposite.

Think of it like a gas tank. A baby whose need for closeness is consistently filled develops an internal sense that the world is safe and that help is available if she needs it. From that foundation, she's more willing to venture out, try new things, and tolerate being apart — because she's confident you'll still be there.

A baby who is held less, and whose bids for closeness are frequently unmet, doesn't become more independent. She becomes more anxious and more clingy — because the tank is never full.

Holding Adelina is not indulgence. It is investment.

questions.question: Scientists who study children who overcome tough circumstances — poverty, illness, loss — looked for the one factor that, above everything else, predicted whether a child would still turn out okay. What did they find?
questions.answer: C) Having at least one adult in their life who was consistently warm, present, and responsive to them
options: A) Natural intelligence or a special talent
B) Getting into a good school early
C) Having at least one adult in their life who was consistently warm, present, and responsive to them
D) Having a strong group of friends
questions.follow_up_context:
This finding — replicated across decades of resilience research — is one of the most hopeful things science has ever shown about children. It doesn't take a perfect home, a perfect parent, or a perfect life. What it takes is *one person* who shows up reliably and loves that child in a way the child can feel.

That person doesn't have to be a parent. It can be a grandparent. A grandmother. A grandfather.

You.

Every time Adelina is with you — and you look at her like she matters, respond when she reaches for you, stay calm when she's upset, make her laugh — you are being that person for her. The research is clear: your presence in her life isn't extra. It is foundational.

questions.question:
Last one — and this one has no right or wrong answer.

Finish this sentence honestly:

'When Adelina is a teenager and something goes wrong in her life, I hope she feels _______ coming to me.'
questions.answer: Open response — any answer is welcome
questions.follow_up_context:
Whatever word you chose — safe, comfortable, easy, unafraid — that feeling doesn't appear out of nowhere at 16. It gets built right now, in moments like the ones this game has been about.

Research on grandparent relationships with teenagers is consistent: the adolescents who confide in their grandparents, who come to them when things go wrong, who trust them with their real lives — those relationships were built in the early years. Through ten thousand small moments of being responded to, seen, and not judged.

Connection-based relationships become the ones teenagers actually show up to.

You already love Adelina more than she will ever fully understand. Now you have the science behind how to make sure she always feels it.
